title: Insurance brokers in apartment sector reported to watchdog
full_text:
Two insurance brokers have been reported to the Australian Securities and Investments Commission this year for failing to disclose conflicts of interest including profit-sharing arrangements with strata managers, following a review by the industry’s independent watchdog.
The Insurance Brokers Code Compliance Committee (IBCCC), the body monitoring and enforcing Australia’s Insurance Brokers Code of Practice, investigated seven insurance brokers who collectively worked with 1088 strata managers representing owners corporations, according its recent report on the sector.
All seven brokers were found to have broken the sector’s code when it came to their dealings with strata management firms.
Strata managers play an increasingly significant role for the owners’ corporations – previously known as body corporates – that are ultimately responsible for the management of apartment buildings.
Typically, strata managers take on administrative tasks such as collecting fees, record-keeping and managing financial affairs – including liaising with brokers regarding a building’s insurance – as well as daily maintenance.
More than 23,000 new apartment completions are forecast for this year and around 64,000 are expected over the three years to 2028, with the demand for strata managers expect to increase accordingly.
But as the sector grows, the potential for conflicts of interest, poor governance and the standard of service delivery in the commercial arrangements for managing apartment towers are coming under increasing scrutiny.
The NSW and Victorian governments are already taking a closer look at arrangements that govern the strata sector while the IBCCC’s review, published at the end of June, is the latest assessment to identify conflicts of interest in the sector.
The watchdog found one of the brokers investigated was working with a strata management firm which was wholly owned by the broker.
As the strata management company had a financial interest in the broker’s business, including a profit-sharing arrangement in place, IBCCC reported the broker to ASIC.
“The code of practice requires more than just disclosure,” IBCCC code compliance general manager Prue Monument told The Australian Financial Review.
“It requires a broker to explain to the client (the owner’s corporation) how they will mitigate or manage any risks associated with the conflict, and then it also requires them to get explicit consent from the client to proceed with the engagement.”
Nine formal breach determinations were issued at the end of the review to all seven brokers, including two brokers referred to ASIC, whose identities have not been disclosed.
“ASIC is aware of the IBCCC report into trust and transparency but cannot comment on individual matters,” an ASIC spokesperson said.
Another two brokers, who dealt with 498 representatives of strata managers between them, were reported by the IBCCC to NSW Fair Trading after provisions in agreements requiring strata managers to solely promote and protect the interests of the broker – a breach of the insurance sector’s code – were discovered.
“The owner’s corporation and the owners of the apartments, they’re the ultimate client,” Monument said. “While the broker has a formal arrangement to work with the strata manager, the broker remains responsible for ensuring that the commitments made in the code are met.
“The brokers [analysed in our review] weren’t doing enough to ensure that commissions and remuneration were being disclosed to the owner’s corporation.”
Australia is racing to build 1.2 million new homes by mid-2029, an ambitious target set by the Albanese government which has spurred a raft of state and federal-level initiatives to encourage the development of higher-density housing across the cities.
In turn, property developers arehttps://www.afr.com/property/residential/nsw-knocks-7-accelerated-projects-out-for-being-too-slow-20260505-p5ztzyfocusing heavily on the construction of apartment buildings to house a growing population, projected to rise from 28 million to 31.5 million by 2035.
One area of concern in the strata apartment sector is the broker commissions and insurance kickbacks paid to strata managers and the extent to which they ought to be disclosed or banned altogether.
Bronwyn Weir, a senior lawyer who specialises in government and building regulatory law, said the problems within the strata industry were much larger than just insurance commissions. There are other kickbacks going on behind closed doors that apartment owners weren’t aware of, but were paying for, she said.
“How many layers of commissions should be being paid for these things because ultimately, it’s the end user that’s footing all those bills,” she told the Financial Review. “[The industry has] become quite toxic, and it is a pretty vulnerable cohort of consumers that are exposed to this kind of behaviour in the strata sector.
“It’s just become a culture that has gone beyond insurance commissions into the way this industry operates, which is completely in the shadows, in terms of what they’re really making out of their clients, and costing the owners.”
State and territory governments, to varying degrees, are cracking down on the once-cottage industry.
A review into Victoria’s strata industry, led by former Victorian Labor consumer affairs minister Marsha Thomson, strata lawyer David McKenzie and economist Karen Chester, recently presented 51 recommendations to the Allan government to help tighten the industry’s regulation in the state.
The Victorian government supported 17, three in part and 26 in principle, but stopped short of backing a complete ban on commission-sharing practices and insurance kickbacks.
In late-2024, the NSW government appointed Angus Abadee as the state’s strata and property services commissioner to help improve the oversight of the industry.
After discovering the state could save around $333 million over 15 years if it were to ban commissions in the industry, the NSW Productivity and Equality Commission endorsed a ban on commissions, following a review into the sector requested by the NSW Minister for Better Regulation and Fair Trading.
But Weir said there was a lack of harmonisation when it came to regulating the sector among the states and territories.
“This is why the industry has got to where it has in terms of the culture and the way that strata managers operate because there really hasn’t been a regulated sector,” she said.
YXJ0aWNsZTpjZWIyYzI3ZS04YjgxLTRhYjAtYTgyZi0yMzIxM2NiZjRhMWU
